source: Mit lÃ¤chelndem Gesichte schaute Siddhartha dem hinweggehenden MÃ¶nche nach. Der Schlaf hatte ihn sehr gestÃ¤rkt, sehr aber quÃ¤lte ihn der Hunger, denn er hatte nun zwei Tage nichts gegessen, und lange war die Zeit vorÃ¼ber, da er hart gegen den Hunger gewesen war. Mit Kummer, und doch auch mit Lachen, gedachte er jener Zeit. Damals, so erinnerte er sich, hatte er sich vor Kamala dreier Dinge gerÃ¼hmt, hatte drei edle und unÃ¼berwindliche KÃ¼nste gekonnt: Fasten â?Warten â?Denken. Dies war sein Besitz gewesen, seine Macht und Kraft, sein fester Stab, in den fleiÃigen, mÃ¼hseligen Jahren seiner Jugend hatte er diese drei KÃ¼nste gelernt, nichts anderes. Und nun hatten sie ihn verlassen, keine von ihnen war mehr sein, nicht Fasten, nicht Warten, nicht Denken. Um das Elendeste hatte er sie hingegeben, um das VergÃ¤nglichste, um SinnenIust, um Wohlleben, um Reichtum! Seltsam war es ihm in der Tat ergangen. Und jetzt, so schien es, jetzt war er wirklich ein Kindermensch geworden.
translation_a: With a smiling face, Siddhartha watched the departing monk. Sleep had greatly strengthened him, but hunger tormented him greatly, for he had not eaten for two days, and the time was long gone when he had been hard against hunger. With sorrow, and yet also with laughter, he thought of that time. At that time, he recalled, he had boasted three things to Kamala, had mastered three noble and insurmountable arts: Fasting - Waiting - Thinking. This had been his possession, his might and strength, his steady staff, in the busy, toilsome years of his youth he had learned these three arts, nothing else. And now they had left him, none of them was his anymore, not fasting, not waiting, not thinking. He had given her up for what was most miserable, for what was most perishable, for sensual pleasure, for a good life, for riches! It had indeed been strange for him. And now, it seemed, he really had become a childish man.
translation_b: With a smiling face, Siddhartha peered after the departing monk. Sleep had greatly strengthened him, but hunger now greatly tormented him, for he had not eaten in two days, and long past was the time when he had been hard toward hunger. With grief, and yet with laughter too, he thought back to that time. He recalled that he had boasted of three things to Kamala; he had been master of three noble and invincible arts: fastingâwaitingâthinking. That had been his property, his might and strength, his solid staff. In the zealous, arduous years of his youth he had learned those three arts, and nothing else. And now they had abandoned him; none of them were his anymore: not fasting, not waiting, not thinking. He had given them away for the most wretched things, the most ephemeral things, for sensuality, for luxury, for wealth! His life had indeed been bizarre. And now, so it seemed, he had truly become a child person.
comment: The first translation is too literal and doesn't quite show understanding of the original ("had become a childish man" - that is not what the original intended to say, the second translation gets this better). The second translation uses better words overall and also better expressions - "His life had indeed been bizarre" is a more accurate and also much better-sounding sentence than "It had indeed been strange for him." The second translation features a better mastery of language and a better representation of the original.

source: PlÃ¶tzlich zuckte Hans Castorp geÃ¤rgert und beleidigt zusammen. Eine TÃ¼r war zugefallen, es war die TÃ¼r links vorn, die gleich in die Halle fÃ¼hrte, â?jemand hatte sie zufallen lassen oder gar hinter sich ins SchloÃ geworfen, und das war ein GerÃ¤usch, das Hans Castorp auf den Tod nicht leiden konnte, das er von jeher gehaÃt hatte. Vielleicht beruhte dieser HaÃ auf Erziehung, vielleicht auf angeborener Idiosynkrasie, â?genug, er verabscheute das TÃ¼renwerfen und hÃ¤tte jeden schlagen kÃ¶nnen, der es sich vor seinen Ohren zuschulden kommen lieÃ. In diesem Fall war die TÃ¼r obendrein mit kleinen Glasscheiben gefÃ¼llt, und das verstÃ¤rkte den Chok: es war ein Schmettern und Klirren. Pfui, dachte Hans Castorp wÃ¼tend, was ist denn das fÃ¼r eine verdammte Schlamperei! Da Ã¼brigens in demselben Augenblick die NÃ¤hterin das Wort an ihn richtete, so hatte er keine Zeit, festzustellen, wer der MissetÃ¤ter gewesen sei. Doch standen Falten zwischen seinen blonden Brauen, und sein Gesicht war peinlich verzerrt, wÃ¤hrend er der NÃ¤hterin antwortete.
translation_a: Suddenly Hans Castorp winced, annoyed and offended. A door had slammed shut, it was the front left door that led straight into the hall - someone had let it shut or even slammed it behind them, and that was a noise that Hans Castorp couldn't stand, that he had always hated. Perhaps that hatred was from education, perhaps from innate idiosyncrasyâenough he detested door-smashing, and could have punched anyone who threw it in front of his ears. In this case the door was also filled with small panes of glass, and that added to the shock: it was a smash and clink. Pfui, thought Hans Castorp angrily, what kind of damned sloppiness is that! Incidentally, since the seamstress spoke to him at the same moment, he had no time to establish who the culprit was. But there were lines between his blond brows and his face was awkwardly contorted as he answered the seamstress.
translation_b: Suddenly Hans Castorp flinchedâhe was annoyed and offended. A door, the one to his left that led to the lobby, had banged shutâsomeone had simply let it slam, or perhaps even slammed it intentionally, and that was a noise that Hans Castorp absolutely could not tolerate, he had always hated it. Perhaps it was a learned dislike, perhaps an inborn idiosyncrasyâ?whatever it was, he abhorred banging doors and could have slapped anyone guilty of slamming one within earshot. In this case, the door was divided into little glass panes, which only heightened the shock: it was a bang and a rattle. âDamn it,â?Hans Castorp thought angrily, âwhat kind of sloppiness is that?â?Since the seamstress had said something to him at the same moment, he had no chance to determine who the malefactor was. But as he answered the seamstress, there were deep furrows between his blond eyebrows and his face was wrenched with distress.
comment: The first translation uses less good sentence structure, some parts sound a bit awkward. It also for some reason still contains the German word "Pfui" instead of its translation. The second translation sounds much smoother, it uses better sentence structure and also better words and expressions (for example "within earshot" vs. "in front of his ears". While the first translation tends to be too literal in places, the second focuses on the meaning and sounds more natural and sophisticated.

source: Siddhartha dankte und nahm an, und wohnte nun im Hause des HÃ¤ndlers. Kleider wurden ihm gebracht, und Schuhe, und ein Diener bereitete ihm tÃ¤glich das Bad. Zweimal am Tage wurde eine reichliche Mahlzeit aufgetragen, Siddhartha aber aÃ nur einmal am Tage, und aÃ weder Fleisch noch trank er Wein. Kamaswami erzÃ¤hlte ihm von seinem Handel, zeigte ihm Waren und Magazine, zeigte ihm Berechnungen. Vieles Neue lernte Siddhartha kennen, er hÃ¶rte viel und sprach wenig. Und der Worte Kamalas eingedenk, ordnete er sich niemals dem Kaufmanne unter, zwang ihn, dass er ihn als seinesgleichen, ja als mehr denn seinesgleichen behandle. Kamaswami betrieb seine GeschÃ¤fte mit Sorglichkeit und oft mit Leidenschaft, Siddhartha aber betrachtete dies alles wie ein Spiel, dessen Regeln genau zu lernen er bemÃ¼ht war, dessen Inhalt aber sein Herz nicht berÃ¼hrte.
translation_a: Siddhartha thanked him and accepted the invitation and now he lived in the merchantâs home. Clothes were brought to him, and shoes, and a servant prepared his daily bath. Twice a day a copious meal was served, but Siddhartha ate only once a day, and he ate no meat and drank no wine. Kamaswami told him about his business, he showed him wares and warehouses, showed him accounts. Siddhartha learned a lot of new things, he heard much and said little. And mindful of Kamalaâs words, he never submitted to the merchant, he forced him to treat Siddhartha as his peer, indeed more than his peer. Kamaswami conducted his business with care and often with passion, but Siddhartha treated it all as a game, whose rules he strove to learn precisely, but whose content did not touch his heart.
translation_b: Siddhartha thanked and accepted, and now lived in the dealer's house. Clothes were brought to him, and shoes, and a servant prepared his bath every day. A large meal was served twice a day, but Siddhartha only ate once a day and neither ate meat nor drank wine. Kamaswami told him about his trade, showed him goods and magazines, showed him calculations. Siddhartha got to know many new things, he heard a lot and spoke little. And remembering the words of Kamala, he never submitted himself to the merchant, compelled him to treat him as his equal, yea, as more than his equal. Kamaswami went about his business with care and often with passion, but Siddhartha looked at it all like a game, the rules of which he tried hard to learn but whose content did not touch his heart.
comment: Neither translation contains errors or badly chosen words, but the first translation is more polished. It is a close call between them, both are fine, but the first translation uses better words (merchant, copious, strove) and also better expressions. It is simply a better read.

source: Gestern sind wir hier angelangt. Der Gesandte ist unpaÃ und wird sich also einige Tage einhalten. Wenn er nur nicht so unhold wÃ¤re, wÃ¤r' alles gut. Ich merke, ich merke, das Schicksal hat mir harte PrÃ¼fungen zugedacht. Doch gutes Muts! Ein leichter Sinn trÃ¤gt alles! Ein leichter Sinn? Das macht mich zu lachen, wie das Wort in meine Feder kommt. O ein biÃchen leichteres Blut wÃ¼rde mich zum GlÃ¼cklichsten unter der Sonne machen. Was! da, wo andere mit ihrem biÃchen Kraft und Talent vor mir in behaglicher SelbstgefÃ¤lligkeit herumschwadronieren, verzweifle ich an meiner Kraft, an meinen Gaben? Guter Gott, der du mir das alles schenktest, warum hieltest du nicht die HÃ¤lfte zurÃ¼ck und gabst mir Selbstvertrauen und GenÃ¼gsamkeit?
translation_a: We arrived here yesterday. The ambassador is indisposed and will not be going out for a few days. If only he were not so unpleasant, things would be a great deal easier. But fate has many trials in store for meâ?I know it, I know it. However, let us not lose courage, for a light heart can bear all things. A light heart? I have to laugh. How could I write such words? Ah yes, a little lighter blood could make me the happiest man on earth! Come, come, Werther! How can you doubt your strength, your gifts, when others complacently parade their puny strength and talents? Dear God, who hath bestowed all this upon me, why didst Thou not withhold a half of it and give me instead faith in myself and a modest capacity for contentment?
translation_b: We got here yesterday. The envoy is impertinent and will therefore hold off for a few days. If only he weren't so rude, everything would be fine. I see, I see, fate has given me tough trials. But good cheer! A light mind carries everything! An easy sense? This makes me laugh as the word comes into my pen. Oh, a little lighter blood would make me the happiest thing under the sun. What! there, where others with their little strength and talent babble about in comfortable self-satisfaction, do I despair of my strength, of my gifts? Good God, who gave me all this, why didn't you hold back half and give me confidence and contentment?
comment: The second translation contains quite a big mistake ("impertinent" instead of "indisposed") and also uses somewhat less sophisticated wording and sentence structure. It also translated "leichter Sinn" with "light mind" once and then "easy sense", though the same term should be used both times, as indicated by the original and the context. The first translation deviates a bit too much (for my taste) from the original in the penultimate sentence, but emulates sense and style well by doing so. In general it is a better representation of the original.

source: Sieben Tische standen im Speisesaal, die meisten in LÃ¤ngsrichtung, nur zwei in die Quere. Es waren grÃ¶Ãere Tafeln, fÃ¼r zehn Personen jede, wenn auch die Gedecke nicht Ã¼berall vollzÃ¤hlig waren. Nur ein paar Schritte schrÃ¤g in den Saal hinein, und Hans Castorp war schon an seinem Platz: er war ihm an der Schmalseite des Tisches bereitet, der mitten vorn stand, zwischen den beiden querstehenden. Aufrecht hinter seinem Stuhle, verbeugte Hans Castorp sich steif und freundlich gegen die Tischgenossen, mit denen Joachim ihn zeremoniell bekannt machte, und die er kaum sah, geschweige, daÃ ihm ihre Namen ins BewuÃtsein gedrungen wÃ¤ren. Einzig Frau StÃ¶hrs Person und Namen faÃte er auf, und daÃ sie ein rotes Gesicht und fettige aschblonde Haare hatte. Man konnte ihr die Bildungsschnitzer wohl zutrauen, so stÃ¶rrisch unwissend war ihr Gesichtsausdruck. Dann setzte er sich und nahm beifÃ¤llig wahr, daÃ man das erste FrÃ¼hstÃ¼ck hier als eine ernste Mahlzeit behandelte.
translation_a: There were seven tables in the dining room, most of them lengthwise, only two across. There were larger tables, for ten people each, even if the covers were not all full. Only a few steps diagonally into the hall, and Hans Castorp was already in his place: he was ready for him on the narrow side of the table, which was standing in the middle in front, between the two people standing at right angles. Standing upright behind his chair, Hans Castorp bowed stiffly and politely to the table companions, to whom Joachim ceremonially introduced him, and whom he scarcely saw, let alone that their names had penetrated his consciousness. All he understood was Frau Stohr's person and name, and that she had a red face and greasy, ash-blonde hair. One could well credit her with the educational blunders, her facial expression was so stubbornly ignorant. Then he sat down and noted with approval that the first breakfast here was treated as a serious meal.
translation_b: There were seven tables in the dining hall, five placed lengthwise, only two crosswise. The tables were large, with room for ten persons at each, although not all the places had been set. They took only a few steps diagonally across the room, and Hans Castorp found that he was already at the place set for him, at the end of the middle table toward the front of the room, halfway between the two crosswise tables. Standing erect behind his chair, Hans Castorp bowed stiffly and cordially to his tablemates as Joachim formally introduced them, although he barely looked at them, let alone made a conscious note of their names. The only face and name he put together was Frau StÃ¶hr, noticing her red face and oily, ash-blond hair, and an expression of such willful ignorance that it was easy to believe her guilty of howling gaffes. Then he sat down and noted approvingly that early breakfast here was a serious meal.
comment: The second translatio is much better than the first. The first makes several mistakes (for example "he" for the set place, using the word "people" when the text is referring to tables). It also uses "politely" when it should be "cordially" or "friendly" and the wording and sentence structures are occasionally awkward. The second translation used appropriate language and represents the style of the original much better.

source: Nicht doch, so weit wollte Hans Castorp nicht gehen. Er unterwarf sich der Kur, obgleich er sie ridikÃ¼l und ehrlos fand. Diese Impfungen mit sich selbst wollten ihm als eine abscheulich freudlose Diversion erscheinen, als ein inzestuÃ¶ser Greuel von Ich zu Ich, frucht- und hoffnungslos in seinem Wesen. So urteilte seine hypochondrische Unbelehrtheit, die nur im Punkte der Unfruchtbarkeit â?und in diesem freilich vollkommen â?recht behielt. Die Diversion erstreckte sich Ã¼ber Wochen. Sie schien zuweilen zu schaden â?was selbstverstÃ¤ndlich auf Irrtum beruhen muÃte â? zuweilen auch zu nÃ¼tzen, was sich dann aber gleichfalls als Irrtum herausstellte. Das Ergebnis war Null, ohne bei Namen genannt und ausdrÃ¼cklich verkÃ¼ndigt zu werden. Die Unternehmung verlief im Sande, und Hans Castorp fuhr fort, Patience zu legen â?Augâ?in Auge mit dem DÃ¤mon, dessen zÃ¼gelloser Herrschaft fÃ¼r sein GefÃ¼hl ein Ende mit Schrecken bevorstand.
translation_a: No, Hans Castorp didn't want to go that far. He submitted to the cure, although he found it ridiculous and dishonorable. These inoculations with himself wanted to appear to him as a hideously joyless diversion, an incestuous ego-to-ego abomination, fruitless and hopeless in his essence. That was the judgment of his hypochondriac ignorance, which was only right on the point of infertility â?and in this, of course, completely. The diversion lasted for weeks. Sometimes it seemed to do harm - which of course had to be based on error - and sometimes to help, which then also turned out to be an error. The result was zero, without being named and expressly proclaimed. The venture fizzled, and Hans Castorp continued to play solitaire face-to-face with the demon whose rampant rule he felt was ending in terror.
translation_b: Well, Hans Castorp was not willing to go quite that far. But he submitted to the therapy, though he found it ridiculous and disgraceful. These vaccinations of himself with himself could be no more than a disgustingly joyless diversion, a self-to-self incestuous abomination, an infertile, hopeless enterprise inside his own body. That was the voice of ignorant hypochondria, which proved correct only as to infertilityâthough, to be sure, completely correct in that one point. The diversion lasted several weeks. At times it seemed to make him illâthough, of course, he had to be in error about thatâat times it seemed to help; but that turned out to be an error as well. The result was zero, though that was never expressly announced. The enterprise fizzled out, and Hans Castorp went on playing solitaireâeye to eye with the demon whose unrestrained power he could not help feeling was sure to come to some horrible end.
comment: The second translation uses better language and sentence structure overall. Neither translation makes mistakes or has overly awkward wording, but the second transltion just reads smoother and more sophisticated. It also emulated the style of the original better.

source: Nun war denn also der Plan eines Ausflugs zum Wasserfall an der Tagesordnung, â?Peeperkorn selbst hatte das Ziel bestimmt, und er fÃ¼hlte sich rÃ¼stig zu dem Unternehmen. Es war der dritte Tag nach einem Quartananfall; Mynheer lieÃ wissen, daÃ er ihn zu nutzen wÃ¼nsche. Zwar war er zu den ersten Mahlzeiten des Tages nicht im Speisesaal erschienen, sondern hatte sie, wie in letzter Zeit sehr hÃ¤ufig, zusammen mit Madame Chauchat in seinem Salon eingenommen; aber schon beim ersten FrÃ¼hstÃ¼ck hatte Hans Castorp durch den hinkenden Concierge Order empfangen, sich eine Stunde nach dem Mittagessen zu einer Spazierfahrt bereitzuhalten, ferner, diesen Befehl an die Herren Ferge und Wehsal weiterzugeben, auch Settembrini und Naphta zu benachrichtigen, daÃ man bei ihnen vorfahren werde, und endlich fÃ¼r die Bestellung zweier Landauer auf drei Uhr Sorge zu tragen.
translation_a: But the plan for an excursion to the waterfall was now on the agendaâPeeperkorn himself had selected the goal, and he felt up to the effort. It was the third day after a quartan attack; Mynheer let it be known he wished to take advantage of the fact. He had not appeared in the dining hall for the first meals of the day, but had taken them, as he often did of late, with Madame Chauchat in his parlor. Nevertheless, as Hans Castorp made his way to first breakfast, he was greeted by the limping concierge and told to be ready for a drive one hour after dinner; he was also to pass the order on to Herr Ferge and Herr Wehsal, likewise to notify Settembrini and Naphta that they would be picked up, and, finally, was given the task of ordering two landaus for three oâclock that afternoon.
translation_b: So now the plan of a trip to the falls was on the order of the day--Peeperkorn himself had chosen the destination, and he felt fit for the enterprise. It was the third day after a quartan bout; Mynheer indicated that he wished to use it. It is true that he had not appeared in the dining-room for the first meals of the day, but had eaten them in his drawing-room with Madame Chauchat, as he had done very often lately; but already at the first breakfast Hans Castorp had received orders from the limping concierge to be ready for a drive an hour after lunch, and also to pass this order on to Messrs. Ferge and Wehsal, and also to notify Settembrini and Naphta that we were to drive up to them and finally to take care of ordering two landau at three o'clock.
comment: The first translation starts off worse than the second - the "but" in the first sentence is less correct than the "so" and "goal" is the wrong word, the second translation correctly uses "destination". However, the first translation then improves while the second translation gets weaker, for example by using two "but" after another in sentence three, whereas the first translation sounds much better with "but" and then "Nevertheless". The second translation also uses the French "Messrs", which does is not used in the original and does make no sense when translating a German text unless it is also used in the original. It also makes a mistake in the last sentence ("that we were to drive up to them") so in total is not very convincing.

source: Sie standen also alle steif aufrecht an unserem Tisch: Hans Castorp und die beiden Gegner und ihnen gegenÃ¼ber Ferge und Wehsal. Alle fÃ¼nf waren sie blaÃ, mit erweiterten Augen und zuckenden MÃ¼ndern. HÃ¤tten nicht die drei Unbeteiligten den Versuch machen kÃ¶nnen, beschwichtigend einzuwirken, mit einem Scherzwort die Spannung zu lÃ¶sen, durch irgendein menschliches Zureden alles zum Guten zu wenden? Sie unternahmen ihn nicht, diesen Versuch. Die inneren UmstÃ¤nde hinderten sie daran. Sie standen und bebten, und unwillkÃ¼rlich ballten ihre HÃ¤nde sich zu FÃ¤usten. Selbst A. K. Ferge, dem alles HÃ¶here erklÃ¤rtermaÃen vÃ¶llig fern lag und der von vornherein gÃ¤nzlich darauf verzichtete, die Tragweite des Streites zu ermessen, â?auch er war Ã¼berzeugt, daÃ es hier auf Biegen und Brechen gehe, und daÃ man, selbst mit hingerissen, nichts tun kÃ¶nne, als den Dingen ihren Lauf zu lassen. Sein gutmÃ¼tiger Schnurrbartbausch wanderte heftig auf und nieder.
translation_a: So they all stood stiffly upright at our table: Hans Castorp and the two opponents and opposite them Ferge and Wehsal. All five of them were pale, with dilated eyes and twitching mouths. Couldn't the three bystanders have tried to calm things down, to ease the tension with a joke, to turn everything around with some human persuasion? They didn't make that attempt. Internal circumstances prevented her from doing so. They stood and trembled, and involuntarily their hands clenched into fists. Even AK Ferge, to whom anything higher was avowedly completely remote and who from the outset completely refrained from assessing the scope of the dispute, - he too was convinced that it was a matter of hook or buck here, and that one, even enraptured, could do nothing can do than let things take their course. His good-natured puff of mustache wandered violently up and down.
translation_b: And now everyone was standing stiffly beside our table: Hans Castorp and the two opponents, and facing them Ferge and Wehsal. All five had turned paleâeyes wide, mouths twitching. Might not the three bystanders have attempted to calm things down, to break the tension with a witty remark, to turn the affair around with some friendly words of advice? They did not make the attempt. Their own inner states prevented them from trying. They stood there trembling, instinctively clenching their hands into fists. Even A. K. Ferge, for whom all higher things were avowedly foreign and who from the start refused to anticipate the full consequences of this disputeâhe, too, was convinced that they had to be prepared for the worst and that having been caught up in it, he now could do nothing but let matters take their course. His good-natured bushy moustache bobbed violently up and down.
comment: The second translation used muc better language and a more sophisticated style. It ready more smoothly than the first translation. The first translation contains some awkwardly worded passages and also mistake (such as "even enraptured" and a wrong pronoun).

source: Recht vergebens, versetzte Naphta, rufe Herr Settembrini die Einfalt jener morgendlichen Zeiten zu seiner Hilfe auf, â?die Siegerin, die ihre SchÃ¶pferkraft noch in der DÃ¤monisierung des Ãberwundenen bewÃ¤hrt habe. Ãbrigens seien die Lehrer der jungen Kirche nicht mÃ¼de geworden, vor den LÃ¼gen der alten Philosophen und Dichter zu warnen, insonderheit davor, sich mit der Ã¼ppigen Beredsamkeit des Virgil zu beflecken, und heute, wo wieder ein Zeitalter zu Grabe sinke, abermals ein proletarischer Morgen tage, sei wahrhaftig die Stunde gÃ¼nstig, ihnen nachzufÃ¼hlen! So mÃ¶ge denn, um alles zu beantworten, Herr Lodovico auch Ã¼berzeugt sein, daÃ er, Redner, sein biÃchen bÃ¼rgerliche BeschÃ¤ftigung, worauf jener anzuspielen die GÃ¼te gehabt habe, mit aller gebotenen reservatio mentalis betreibe und sich nicht ohne Ironie in einen klassisch-rhetorischen Erziehungsbetrieb einordne, dessen Lebensdauer ein Sanguiniker allenfalls noch nach Jahrzehnten berechnen mÃ¶ge.
translation_a: It was in vain, replied Naphta, that M. Settembrini called upon the simplicity of those early mornings to help himâthe victor who had proved her creative powers by demonizing the vanquished. Incidentally, the teachers of the early church never tired of warning against the lies of the old philosophers and poets, especially against smearing themselves with the lavish eloquence of Virgil, and today, when another age is sinking to the grave, another proletarian tomorrow days, truly be the right hour to empathize with them! So, in order to answer everything, Mr. Lodovico may also be convinced that he, a speaker, pursues his bit of civil occupation, to which he was kind enough to allude, with all the necessary reservations and not without irony in a classical-rhetorical educational establishment classify, whose lifespan a sanguine may at best calculate after decades.
translation_b: It was all in vain, Naphta responded, that Herr Settembrini appealed to the naivetÃ© of those victorious morning years of Christianityâwhich had only proved its creative force by making demons of those it conquered. Moreover, the teachers of the early Church had never wearied of warning people against the lies of the philosophers and poets of antiquity, and in particular against sullying themselves with the lush eloquence of a Virgil. Indeed, the present day, when once again one age was heading for the grave even as a proletarian age was dawning, was a particularly appropriate time for respecting such sentiments. And as for the rest of his charge, Herr Lodovico could be sure that he, Naphta, pursued his modicum of bourgeois activity, to which the former had been kind enough to allude, with all due reservatio mentalis and that he recognized a certain irony in having found a niche in an educational institution devoted to classical rhetoric, a pedagogy whose life span even the most sanguine would estimate only in decades.
comment: The first translation is quite bad. It reads like a machine translation, very literal without any attention to the sense of the words, which then leads to nonsensical passages like this: "another proletarian tomorrow days", where the wrong meaning of "Morgen" is used (tomorrow instead of morning) as well as the wrong meaning of "tage" (days instead of dawns). This is not the only nonsensical passage. The second translation is not only correct but also uses much better language, sufficiently sophisticated and fitting with the style of the original.

source: Â»NeinÂ«, sagte Hans Castorp, als er wieder an seinem Tischende zwischen der NÃ¤hterin und der EnglÃ¤nderin Platz genommen und ergeben seine Serviette entfaltet hatte, obgleich {106}er noch so schwer belastet vom ersten FrÃ¼hstÃ¼ck war. Â»NeinÂ«, sagte er, Â»Gott steh mir bei, Milch kann ich Ã¼berhaupt nicht trinken und am wenigsten jetzt. Ist nicht vielleicht Porter da?Â« Und er wandte sich zuerst hÃ¶flich und zart an die Zwergin mit dieser Frage. Leider war keiner da. Aber sie versprach, Kulmbacher Bier zu bringen und brachte es auch. Es war dick, schwarz, braunschaumig und ersetzte den Porter aufs beste. Hans Castorp trank durstig davon aus einem hohen Halbliterglase. Er aÃ kalten Aufschnitt dazu auf RÃ¶stbrot. Wieder war Haferbrei aufgestellt und wieder viel Butter und Obst. Er lieÃ wenigstens seine Augen darauf ruhen, da er nicht fÃ¤hig war, sich davon zuzufÃ¼hren. Auch betrachtete er die GÃ¤steschaft, â?die Massen begannen sich fÃ¼r ihn zu teilen; Einzelpersonen traten hervor.
translation_a: "No," said Hans Castorp, when he had sat down again at his end of the table between the seamstress and the Englishwoman and devotedly unfolded his serviette, although he was still so heavily burdened from the first breakfast. 'No,' he said, 'God help me, I can't drink milk at all, least of all now. Isn't Porter there, perhaps?' And he addressed the question first to the dwarf, politely and delicately. Unfortunately nobody was there. But she promised to bring Kulmbacher beer and she did. It was thick, black, frothy brown and a perfect substitute for the porter. Thirsty, Hans Castorp drank from a tall half-litre glass. He ate cold cold cuts on toast. There was porridge again and lots of butter and fruit again. He at least kept his eyes on it, unable to feed himself from it. He also looked at the guests - the masses began to part for him; Individuals emerged.
translation_b: âNo,â?Hans Castorp said, taking his seat again at the end of the table between the seamstress and the Englishwoman and conscientiously unfolding his napkinâalthough he was still weighed down by his first breakfast. âNo,â?he said, âGod help me, but I do not drink milk, and certainly not now. Is there some porter, perhaps?â?And he turned to ask this question politely and gently of the dwarf. There was no porter, unfortunately. But she promised him some Kulmbach beer, and indeed she brought it. It was thick and black, with a foamy brown head, and was an excellent substitute for porter. Hans Castorp drank thirstily from the tall pint glass. He ate cold cuts on toast. There was more oatmeal on the table and lots of butter and fruit again. He at least let his eyes pass over it all, since he was incapable of helping himself to any of it. And he observed the other guests, tooâthe crowd was beginning to sort itself out for him and individuals were emerging.
comment: The first translation contains several mistakes, such as wrong capitalization, mistaking a beverage ("porter") for a person. The second translation also contains a mistake in letting Castop say "I do not drink milk" instead of "I can't drink milk" but compared to the mistakes in the first translations it's minor. It also uses better wording and reads more smoothly overall.
